title: Warner Bros fights Paramount's $108bn hostile offer | FT #shorts

transcript.text: Warner Bros. Discovery sent a clear
message to its shareholders on December
17. Trust us, the best buyer of the
storied Hollywood company is Netflix,
not Paramount. Paramount is led by the
billionaire son of Oracle's founder,
David Ellison. He put forward a hostile
108 billion bid last week to buy Warner
Bros., but the Warner board called that
offer inferior and too risky for
investors. Why? They're telling Warner
investors that the Ellison family, the
second richest in America, can't be
trusted to back the financing of the
transaction, despite the Ellison's
offering to backs stop the entire deal
with their family trust. That is worth
between 200 and $250 billion in Oracle
stock. David Ellison rejected the Warner
claims. Paramount has essentially
accused Warner of playing games and
ending the bid abruptly to favor Netflix
who bid $83 billion to buy the streaming
and studio assets that includes HBO and
it excludes the TV assets like CNN.
What's next? Paramount is asking Warner
investors to tender their shares in
favor of their $30 per share offer in
cash. They have until January 8th to do
so unless Warner extends the tender
period. This battle is now one of the
biggest corporate fights in Hollywood
history and it could reshape major
studios and how movies get made and
streamed. Follow for the latest on this
story as it unfolds and drop a comment.
Who do you think will win, Paramount or
Netflix?
